title: ETF vs Index Fund Comparison Guide | Westmount Fundamentals
description: ETFs and index funds track the same benchmarks — so why does one cost you more in taxes? The differences that actually matter for your portfolio.

schemas.headline: ETF vs Index Fund Comparison Guide
schemas.description: A comprehensive educational resource comparing ETFs and index funds based on expense ratios, tax efficiency, trading flexibility, minimum investments, and performance differences.

mainEntity.name: What is the main difference between an ETF and an Index Fund?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main difference is how they trade. ETFs trade on an exchange like a stock, meaning their price fluctuates throughout the day and you can buy/sell anytime the market is open. Index funds (structured as mutual funds) trade only once a day at the closing net asset value (NAV) price.

mainEntity.name: Which is more tax-efficient: an ETF or an Index Fund?

acceptedAnswer.text: ETFs are generally more tax-efficient due to their unique creation and redemption mechanism, which avoids capital gains distributions. Vanguard index funds are an exception, as they hold a patent allowing their mutual funds to share the tax efficiency of their ETF counterparts.

mainEntity.name: Can I automatically reinvest dividends with both?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Most brokerages allow automatic dividend reinvestment (DRIP) for both ETFs and mutual funds. However, mutual funds natively support reinvestment into fractional shares, whereas ETF DRIP capabilities depend on your specific broker supporting fractional ETF shares.
